pattern: Strip away which specific letter maps to which count and compare only the two shape-defining multisets: the set of letters used and the multiset of their frequencies.
hints: Swapping which letter carries which count never changes the set of letters present or how often each count occurs — so compare those two properties instead of the raw strings.
Build a frequency count per string, then check both strings use exactly the same set of distinct letters.
Finally check that the sorted list of frequencies (not which letter owns them) is identical between the two strings.

edge_inputs.description: same letters and same frequency multiset but mapped to different letters
same letters used, but the frequency multiset differs
completely different letter sets, same lengths
one string is a strict prefix-length subset of letters missing one, differing lengths
an exact permutation of the same string
long string built from a single repeated letter compared against itself

stress.description: random strings over a small alphabet, then a guaranteed-close variant built by relabeling letters
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    length = max(1, min(size, 20000))
    alphabet = 'abcde'
    word1 = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length))
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        perm = list(alphabet)
        rng.shuffle(perm)
        mapping = dict(zip(alphabet, perm))
        word2 = ''.join(mapping[c] for c in word1)
    else:
        word2 = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(length))
    return [word1, word2]
stress.note: half the time builds a guaranteed-close pair via relabeling, half the time an independent random string
